2.6: hos en morphe theou hyparchon ouch harpagmon hegesato to einai isa theo (hos en morphē theou hyparchōn ouch harpagmon hēgēsato to einai isa theō): the most debated sentence in Philippians. Morphe theou (form/mode of God) is not mere appearance (doxa/eidos) but the actual mode of existence — the divine being's characteristic reality. Harpagmon (HARPAGMOS): the crux. Older interpretation: 'did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped (and held onto)' — the pre-existent Son relinquished divine prerogatives. Newer consensus (N.T. Wright, R. Hoover): 'did not regard equality with God as something to exploit for his own advantage' — already possessing it, he did not use it selfishly. The second reading requires no ontological descent at incarnation — the Son freely chose not to exploit his divine position. Either way, the kenosis (self-emptying, v. 7) is voluntary and constitutes the pattern for Christian ethics (v. 5).
2.9: dio kai ho theos auton hyperypsosen kai echarisato auto to onoma to hyper pan onoma (dio kai ho theos auton hyperypsōsen kai echarisato autō to onoma to hyper pan onoma): 'Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' Hyperypsōsen (super-exalted) is a hapax-legomenon in the NT — the hyper prefix intensifies beyond normal exaltation. The 'name above every name' that is then used in v. 10 is YHWH (Kyrios): by giving Jesus the name Lord-of-all, God applies Isa 45:23 (every knee bows to YHWH) to Jesus. The highest OT claim — universal homage to YHWH alone — is now predicated of the crucified and exalted Jesus.
2.11: kai pasa glossa exomologesetai hoti Kyrios Iesous Christos eis doxan Theou patros (kai pāsa glōssa exomologēsetai hoti Kyrios Iēsous Christos eis doxan Theou patros): 'every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.' The confession Kyrios Iesous Christos is the earliest Christian creed (cf. Rom 10:9; 1 Cor 12:3). Its application of Isa 45:23 to Jesus is the most explicit equation of Jesus with YHWH in the undisputed Pauline letters. The eis doxan Theou patros (to the glory of God the Father) prevents the Christology from displacing the Father: Jesus's lordship is the Father's glory, not a rival to it — a proto-Trinitarian formulation.
2.1: Ei tis oun paraklēsis en Christō, ei ti paramythion agapēs, ei tis koinōnia pneumatos, ei tis splagchna kai oiktirmoi: 'If there is any encouragement (paraklēsis) in Christ, any comfort (paramythion) from love, any participation (koinōnia) in the Spirit, any affection and sympathy (splagchna kai oiktirmoi).' The fourfold conditional assumes all four conditions are real — the Greek idiom treats them as certainly true. Paraklēsis (encouragement, consolation) shares its root with the Paraclete; paramythion (comfort, consolation through words); koinōnia pneumatos (fellowship/sharing of the Spirit). The appeal is to their shared experience of Christ.
2.2: plērōsate mou tēn charan hina to auto phronēte tēn autēn agapēn echontes sympsychoi to hen phronountes: 'complete my joy by being of the same mind (to auto phronēte), having the same love, being in full accord (sympsychoi — one-souled) and of one mind (to hen phronountes).' The double use of phronein (to have the same mindset) frames the unity appeal. Sympsychoi (knit together in soul) — a Pauline hapax. The unity Paul seeks is not merely behavioral conformity but deep shared orientation and affection.
2.3: mēden kat' eritheian mēde kata kenodoxian alla tē tapeinophrosynē allēlous hēgoumenoi hyperechontas heautōn: 'Do nothing from selfish ambition (eritheian) or conceit (kenodoxian, empty glory/vainglory), but in humility (tapeinophrosynē) count others more significant than yourselves.' Tapeinophrosynē (humility, lowliness of mind) was not a virtue in Greco-Roman culture — it was associated with the servile, the defeated, the socially inferior. Paul installs it as the Christian alternative to Roman honor-competition. Hēgoumenoi hyperechontas heautōn: reckoning others as surpassing oneself — a radical counter-cultural posture.
2.4: mē ta heautōn hekastos skopeite alla [kai] ta heterōn hekastos: 'Let each of you look not only to his own interests, but also to the interests of others.' Skopeite (observe, look to, attend to) — the active consideration of others' concerns alongside one's own. This verse provides the ethical frame that the Christ-hymn (vv.6-11) then grounds christologically: the pattern of not-grasping-for-oneself is the Christ-pattern.
2.5: touto phroneite en hymin ho kai en Christō Iēsou: 'Have this mind (touto phroneite) among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus.' The transition to the Christ-hymn: the mindset Paul calls for is already present in the community through Christ. Phronein here introduces the Christological basis for the ethical appeal. The 'mind of Christ' is not an abstract ideal but the attitude concretely embodied in the narrative of vv.6-11.
2.7: alla heauton ekenōsen morphēn doulou labōn en homoiōmati anthrōpōn genomenos: 'but emptied himself (ekenōsen), by taking the form of a servant (morphēn doulou labōn), being born in the likeness (homoiōmati) of men.' Ekenōsen (from kenoō) — to empty, make void. The Kenotic Christology centers on this verb: the divine Son voluntarily relinquished (not his divine nature but) the independent exercise of divine prerogatives, taking instead the form of a slave. Morphēn doulou: the form of a doulos (slave/servant) is the direct antithesis of morphē theou. The Son of God took the social position of the lowest: the slave who has no rights, whose entire existence is defined by another's will.
2.8: kai schēmati heuretheis hōs anthrōpos etapeinōsen heauton genomenos hypēkoos mechri thanatou, thanatou de staurou: 'And being found in human form (schēmati), he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' Schēma (form, appearance, external configuration — as in the schematic of a plan): Christ was found in the outward configuration of a human being. Etapeinōsen heauton (he humbled himself) — active self-humiliation. Mechri thanatou, thanatou de staurou: 'even to death — death on a cross.' The shocking climax: the cross was Rome's most shameful execution, reserved for the lowest criminals and slaves. The Son of God died a slave's death.
2.10: hina en tō onomati Iēsou pan gony kampsē epouraniōn kai epigeiōn kai katachthoniōn: 'so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth.' The three-level cosmos (heavenly/earthly/subterranean — the classic ancient cosmological division: gods/humans/the dead) bows before the name of Jesus. Kampsē (bend, bow down) — the physical act of submission. The citation of Isa 45:23 applies the absolute claim of YHWH's universal sovereignty to the exalted Jesus.
2.12: hōste agapētoi mou kathōs pantote hypēkousate mē hōs en tē parousia mou monon alla nyn pollō mallon en tē apousia mou, meta phobou kai tromou tēn heautōn sōtērian katergazesthe: 'Therefore, my beloved, as you have always obeyed, so now, not only as in my presence (parousia) but much more in my absence (apousia), work out your own salvation (sōtērian katergazesthe) with fear and trembling (phobou kai tromou).' Katergazesthe (work out, accomplish, bring to completion) — not earn but work out the implications of received salvation. Phobos kai tromos: OT language for the awe of divine encounter (Exod 15:16; Ps 2:11). The fear is reverence before the God who is at work (v.13), not fear of loss of salvation.
2.13: ho theos gar estin ho energōn en hymin kai to thelein kai to energein hyper tēs eudokias: 'for it is God who works (ho energōn) in you, both to will (to thelein) and to work (to energein) for his good pleasure (eudokias).' The divine-human cooperation: God works in them (the indicative ground) so they work out their salvation (the imperative response). Even the will to obey is God's gift. Eudokia (good pleasure, benevolent purpose) — God's working has its source in his own gracious purpose, not in the congregation's merit.
2.14: panta poieite chōris gongysmōn kai dialogismōn: 'Do all things without grumbling (gongysmōn) or disputing (dialogismōn).' Gongysmoi (murmurings, grumblings) — the LXX word for Israel's wilderness complaints against Moses and God (Exod 16:7-12; Num 11:1; 14:27). The echo is deliberate: the Philippians are to do the opposite of Israel in the wilderness. Dialogismoi (disputes, questionings, debates) — internal community conflict that undermines the unified mission.
2.15: hina genēsthe amemptoi kai akeraioi tekna theou amōma mesē geneas skolias kai diestrammenēs en hois phainesthē hōs phōstēres en kosmō: 'that you may be blameless (amemptoi) and innocent (akeraioi), children of God without blemish (amōma) in the midst of a crooked and twisted generation, among whom you shine as lights (phōstēres) in the world.' Amōmos (without blemish — the sacrificial standard) combined with phōstēres (lights, luminaries — the stars of Dan 12:3: those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the sky). The congregation is Israel-in-the-wilderness reconstituted as the light-bearing community in the crooked generation.
2.16: logon zōēs epechontes eis kauchēma emoi eis hēmeran Christou, hoti ouk eis kenon edramon oude eis kenon ekopiasa: 'holding fast (epechontes) to the word of life, so that in the day of Christ I may be proud that I did not run in vain (eis kenon edramon) or labor in vain.' The athletic metaphor: edramon (I ran) — Paul's apostolic work as a race (1 Cor 9:24-26; Gal 2:2). The fear is kenon (empty, vain) — that the Philippians' failure would nullify the apostolic effort at the day of Christ.
2.17: alla ei kai spendomai epi tē thysia kai leitourgia tēs pisteōs hymōn chariō kai synchairō pasin hymin: 'Even if I am to be poured out as a drink offering (spendomai) upon the sacrificial offering and service (leitourgia) of your faith, I am glad and rejoice with you all.' Spendomai (I am poured out as a libation) — the Roman and Jewish sacrificial practice of pouring wine on the altar as a drink offering. Paul's potential martyrdom is reframed as a libation poured over the Philippians' faith as the primary sacrifice. The apostle's death would be an additional offering over their living faith-offering.
2.18: to de auto kai hymeis chairete kai synchairete moi: 'Likewise you also should be glad and rejoice with me.' The mutual joy: even if Paul is executed, both he and the Philippians should rejoice — because his death would be a sacrificial act completing the gospel work. Joy is not mood-dependent but stems from the theological reality of what suffering means in Christ.
2.19: elpizō de en kyriō Iēsou Timotheon tacheōs pempsai hymin hina kagō euthymō gnous ta peri hymōn: 'I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I too may be cheered by news of you.' The hope is qualified en kyriō Iēsou (in the Lord Jesus) — Paul's plans are subject to the Lord's will (cf. Jas 4:15). Timothy as emissary: his sending extends Paul's own pastoral concern.
2.20: oudena gar echo isopsychon hostis gnēsiōs ta peri hymōn merimnēsei: 'For I have no one like him (isopsychon — of equal soul), who will be genuinely concerned for your welfare.' Isopsychos (equal-souled, like-minded) — a Pauline hapax. Timothy's genuine (gnēsiōs — legitimately, not pretended) care for the Philippians mirrors Paul's own. The others seek their own interests (v.21), not the things of Christ Jesus — an implicit contrast with the Christ-hymn's self-emptying pattern.
2.21: hoi pantes gar ta heautōn zētousin, ou ta Iesou Christou: 'For they all seek their own interests, not those of Jesus Christ.' The stark contrast to the Christ-hymn's principle (v.4: look not to your own interests) and to Timothy's character. Those seeking their own interests represent the worldly pattern; Timothy represents the Christomorphic pattern.
2.22: tēn de dokimēn autou ginōskete hoti hōs patri teknon syn emoi edouleusen eis to euangelion: 'But you know Timothy's proven worth (dokimē), how as a son with a father he has served with me in the gospel.' Dokimē (proven character, tested quality — from dokimazō: to test by fire). The father-son relationship describes the apprenticeship dynamic of apostolic formation: Timothy learned gospel ministry by serving alongside Paul.
2.23: touton men oun elpizō pempsai hōs an aphidō ta peri eme exautēs: 'I hope therefore to send him just as soon as I see how it will go with me.' The uncertainty about Paul's legal outcome governs the timing. He cannot leave himself until the situation is resolved; Timothy is his advance ambassador.
2.24: pepoitha de en kyriō hoti kai autos tacheōs eleusomai: 'and I trust in the Lord that shortly I myself will come also.' The personal visit anticipated en kyriō — subject to divine permission. Despite imprisonment, Paul anticipates release and reunion with the congregation he loves.
2.25: anagkaion de hēgēsamēn Epaphroditon ton adelphon kai synergon kai systratiōtēn mou hymōn de apostolon kai leitourgon tēs chreias mou pempsai pros hymas: 'I have thought it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus my brother and fellow worker (synergon) and fellow soldier (systratiōtēn), and your messenger (apostolon) and minister (leitourgon) to my need.' The five-fold description of Epaphroditus: brother, co-worker, co-soldier (evoking military partnership), apostolos (sent one — the Philippians' envoy, using the same term as Paul's apostleship), leitourgos (priestly minister — a cultic term for official service). The priestly vocabulary elevates his service into an act of worship.
2.26: epeidē epipothōn ēn pantas hymas kai adēmonōn dioti ēkousate hoti ēsthenēsen: 'for he has been longing for you all and has been distressed because you heard that he was ill.' Epipothōn (longing for) — the same verb used for Paul's longing for the Philippians (1:8). Adēmonōn (distressed, troubled in spirit) — used in Mark 14:33 for Jesus in Gethsemane. Epaphroditus's pastoral concern for the congregation's anxiety about him is itself a Christomorphic trait.
2.27: kai gar ēsthenēsen paraplēsion thanatō alla ho theos ēleēsen auton, ouk auton de monon alla kai eme hina mē lypēn epi lypēn schō: 'Indeed he was ill, near to death. But God had mercy on him, and not only on him but on me also, lest I should have sorrow upon sorrow.' The honest acknowledgment of Epaphroditus's near-fatal illness — prayer does not always prevent suffering, but God showed mercy. The lypēn epi lypēn (sorrow upon sorrow) Paul would have suffered: his own imprisonment-sorrow plus the grief of losing his co-worker.
2.28: spoudaioterōs oun epempsa auton hina idontes auton palin charēte kagō alypoteros ō: 'I am the more eager to send him, therefore, that you may rejoice at seeing him again, and that I may be less anxious.' The mutual joy of reunion: the congregation's rejoicing at Epaphroditus's return becomes Paul's relief. The pastoral economy of shared joy circulates throughout the letter.
2.29: prsdechesthe oun auton en kyriō meta pasēs charas kai tous toioutous entimous echete: 'So receive him in the Lord with all joy (meta pasēs charas), and honor (entimous) such men.' The community is to honor those who have served to the point of death (v.30). Entimos (honored, precious, valued) — the counter-cultural reversal: Paul calls the congregation to honor the servant-martyr rather than the socially powerful.
2.30: hoti dia to ergon Christou mechri thanatou ēngisen paraboleusamenos tē psychē hina anaplērōsē to hymōn hysterēma tēs pros me leitourgias: 'for he nearly died for the work of Christ, risking his life (paraboleusamenos tē psychē) to complete what was lacking (hysterēma) in your service to me.' Paraboleusamenos (risking, gambling with — from parabolos, one who ventures, takes risks): Epaphroditus gambled his life in service. The hysterēma (lack, deficiency) the Philippians could not supply in person because of distance — Epaphroditus supplied it by his presence and service.

3.8: alla men oun kai hegoumai panta zemia einai dia to hyperechon tes gnoseos Christou Iesou tou kyriou mou: 'Indeed, I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord.' Zemia (loss) and skybala (v. 8: dung/rubbish — a deliberately coarse word, the strongest available Greek term for something worthless or repulsive) balance against to hyperechon (the surpassing value). Paul revalues his entire pre-conversion Jewish curriculum vitae — circumcision, Benjaminite lineage, Pharisaic zeal, Torah-righteousness — as skybala compared to the knowing (gnōsis) of Christ. This is not anti-Judaism but a Christological superordination: Christ is worth more than everything else combined.
3.20: hemon de to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei (hēmōn de to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei): 'Our citizenship (politeuma) is in heaven.' Politeuma is a technical term for a community's civic identity — Philippi was a Roman colony whose citizens held Roman citizenship with its legal privileges (Acts 16:21). Paul uses the very term for civic identity to relocate the Philippians' primary belonging: they are residents in a Roman colony but their true politeuma is heavenly. The word also evokes the Jewish diaspora institution of a politeuma — a recognized community of foreigners living under their own governance within a host city (attested in Egypt and Cyrene).
3.1: To loipon, adelphoi mou, chairete en kyriō. Ta auta graphein hymin emoi men ouk oknēron hymin de asphales: 'Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you is no trouble to me and is safe for you.' Chairete en kyriō (rejoice in the Lord) — the letter's thematic summons. The 'same things' may refer to repeated warnings about the Judaizers or to the repeated call to joy. Asphales (safe, firm — a nautical term for securely anchored): the repetition is a safety measure for the congregation's doctrinal stability.
3.2: blepete tous kynas, blepete tous kakous ergatas, blepete tēn katatomēn: 'Look out for the dogs (kynas), look out for the evil workers (kakous ergatas), look out for those who mutilate the flesh (katatomēn).' The triple blepete (look out for, beware) signals urgent warning. Kynas: 'dogs' was a Jewish epithet for Gentiles; Paul inverts it against those who demand Gentile circumcision. Katatomē (mutilation) — a bitter wordplay on peritomē (circumcision): those who insist on circumcision as necessary for salvation are reducing it to mere bodily mutilation without covenant significance.
3.3: hēmeis gar esmen hē peritomē hoi pneumati theou latreuontes kai kauchōmenoi en Christō Iēsou kai ouk en sarki pepoithotes: 'For we are the circumcision (hē peritomē), who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus and put no confidence in the flesh (sarki).' Paul's audacious counter-claim: the true circumcision is the church — those who worship in/by the Spirit, boast in Christ, and place zero confidence in physical or legal credentials. The definition of the covenant people is pneumatological and christological, not ethnic or ritual.
3.4: kaiper egō echōn pepoithēsin kai en sarki. Ei tis dokei allos pepoithenai en sarki, egō mallon: 'though I myself have reason for confidence in the flesh also. If anyone else thinks he has reason for confidence in the flesh, I have more.' The rhetorical synkrisis (comparison): Paul does not dismiss fleshly credentials as if he lacked them, but out-ranks the Judaizing opponents on their own grounds before dismissing those grounds entirely (vv.7-8).
3.5: peritomē oktaēmeros ek genous Israēl phylēs Beniamin Hebraios ex Hebraiōn kata nomon Pharisaios: 'circumcised on the eighth day, of the people of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews; as to the law, a Pharisee.' The pedigree: (1) eighth-day circumcision — Torah-observant birth, not adult conversion; (2) Israel — full ethnic membership; (3) Benjamin — the royal tribe; (4) Hebrew of Hebrews — Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish heritage; (5) Pharisee — the most rigorous Torah movement. This is the curriculum vitae of the highest-status Jewish scholar of Paul's generation.
3.6: kata zēlon diōkōn tēn ekklēsian, kata dikaiosynēn tēn en nomō genomenos amemptos: 'as to zeal, a persecutor of the church; as to righteousness under the law, blameless (amemptos).' The zeal (zēlos) for the Torah that drove Paul to persecute the church (Acts 8:3; 9:1-2; Gal 1:13-14) was itself evidence of his pre-conversion Jewish standing. Amemptos (blameless) — not claiming moral perfection but covenantal compliance: he kept the law's requirements as understood within Pharisaism. This is not self-justification but the inventory of what he has renounced.
3.7: [alla] hatina ēn moi kerdē tauta hēgēmai dia ton Christon zēmian: 'But whatever gain (kerdē) I had, I counted as loss (zēmian) for the sake of Christ.' The accounting metaphor: assets on the credit side of the ledger become debits when Christ is brought into the calculation. Zēmia (loss, damage, financial harm) — the commercial language of profit-and-loss. The entire Jewish credential-portfolio is reclassified as debit.
3.9: kai heurethō en autō mē echōn emēn dikaiosynēn tēn ek nomou alla tēn dia pisteōs Christou tēn ek theou dikaiosynēn epi tē pistei: 'and be found in him, not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ (dia pisteōs Christou), the righteousness from God (ek theou dikaiosynēn) that depends on faith.' The two righteousnesses contrasted: (1) ē emē dikaiosynē ek nomou: my own righteousness from the law — self-generated covenantal standing through legal performance; (2) ē dia pisteōs Christou ek theou: the righteousness through faith in Christ from God — externally given, christologically mediated. Dia pisteōs Christou: either faith in Christ (objective genitive: we trust Christ) or the faithfulness of Christ (subjective genitive: Christ's own covenant faithfulness). Both convey christological dependence.
3.10: tou gnōnai auton kai tēn dynamin tēs anastaseōs autou kai [tēn] koinōnian [tōn] pathēmatōn autou symmorphizomenos tō thanatō autou: 'that I may know him and the power of his resurrection (dynamin tēs anastaseōs autou), and may share his sufferings (koinōnian tōn pathēmatōn autou), becoming like him in his death (symmorphizomenos tō thanatō autou).' The participatory knowledge of Christ: gnōnai (to know — relational, experiential, not merely propositional). The three dimensions: resurrection power, suffering partnership, and death-conformity. Symmorphizomenos (being conformed to, becoming like in form) — the christomorphic process applied to the apostle's dying.
3.11: ei pōs katantēsō eis tēn exanastasin tēn ek nekrōn: 'that by any means possible I may attain the resurrection from the dead (exanastasin ek nekrōn).' Exanastasis (out-resurrection — a compound form unique in the NT) — the resurrection out from among the dead, distinguishing the resurrection of believers from mere resuscitation. The ei pōs (if by any means) is not uncertainty about final salvation but apostolic sobriety: he does not presume but presses toward the goal.
3.12: ouch hoti ēdē elabon ē ēdē teteleiomai diōkō de ei kai katalabō eph' hō kai katelēmphthēn hypo Christou [Iēsou]: 'Not that I have already obtained this or am already perfect (teteleiomai), but I press on (diōkō) to make it my own, because Christ Jesus has made me his own (katelēmphthēn hypo Christou).' The Damascus road as capture: Christ seized Paul first; now Paul presses after Christ. Diōkō (I pursue, press after) — the same verb Paul used for his persecution of the church (v.6); his zeal is redirected from law-enforcement to Christ-pursuit.
3.13: adelphoi egō emauton ou logizomai kateilēphenai hen de, ta men opisō epilanthanomenos tois de emprosthen epekteinomenos: 'Brothers, I do not consider that I have made it my own. But one thing I do: forgetting what lies behind (opisō epilanthanomenos) and straining forward (epekteinomenos) to what lies ahead.' Epekteinomenos (stretching out toward, straining forward) — the image of the runner leaning into the finish line. Forgetting the past: both past failures (pre-conversion sin) and past achievements (the credential-list of vv.5-6) are released to press forward.
3.14: kata skopon diōkō eis to brabeion tēs anō klēseōs tou theou en Christō Iēsou: 'I press on toward the goal (skopon) for the prize (brabeion) of the upward call (anō klēseōs) of God in Christ Jesus.' Skopos (target, goal — from skopeō: to look toward). Brabeion (prize — the wreath awarded the winner, 1 Cor 9:24-25). The upward call of God: the divine summons draws the apostle forward and upward. The prize is defined christologically and eschatologically.
3.15: hosoi oun teleioi touto phronōmen kai ei ti heterōs phroneite kai touto ho theos hymin apokalypsei: 'Let those of us who are mature (teleioi) think this way, and if in anything you think otherwise, God will reveal that also to you.' The ironic use of teleioi (perfect/mature — the term the perfectionist opponents might claim): Paul applies it to those who know they are not yet perfect (v.12). The promise of further divine revelation for those with different views is pastorally generous.
3.16: plēn eis ho ephthasamen tō autō stoichein: 'Only let us hold true to what we have attained.' Stoichein (to walk in line, to march in order) — the military term for keeping ranks. The community is to maintain unified progress in what they have already received, not to regress or fracture over contested points.
3.17: symmimētai mou ginesthe adelphoi kai skopette tous houtōs peripatountas kathōs echete typon hēmas: 'Brothers, join in imitating me (symmimētai), and keep your eyes on those who walk according to the example you have in us.' Symmimētai (fellow-imitators, co-imitators — a Pauline hapax): not 'imitate me' alone but 'imitate me together, in company with others who do the same.' The typos (type, pattern, example) — apostolic life as a visible pattern for community formation.
3.18: polloi gar peripatousin hous pollakis elegon hymin nyn de kai klaiōn legō tous echthrous tou staurou tou Christou: 'For many, of whom I have often told you and now tell you even with tears, walk as enemies of the cross of Christ (echthrous tou staurou tou Christou).' The pathos of apostolic warning: Paul weeps. Echthrous tou staurou (enemies of the cross): not theological enemies of the cross-doctrine but those whose manner of life (peripatousin) contradicts the cross's cruciform pattern. Their identity is given in v.19.
3.19: hōn to telos apōleia, hōn ho theos hē koilia kai hē doxa en tē aischynē autōn, hoi ta epigeia phronountes: 'Their end is destruction (apōleia), their god is their belly (koilia), and they glory in their shame, with minds set on earthly things (ta epigeia phronountes).' The three marks of the enemies of the cross: (1) god = belly — appetite/desire rules (libertine interpretation) or food laws elevated to salvation-status (rigorist interpretation); (2) glory in shame — inverting the honor-shame calculus; (3) ta epigeia phronountes — the exact antithesis of the heavenly citizenship (v.20) and the cross's upward call (v.14).
3.21: hos metaschēmatisei to sōma tēs tapeinōseōs hēmōn symmorphon tō sōmati tēs doxēs autou kata tēn energeian tou dynasthai auton kai hypotaxai autō ta panta: 'who will transform (metaschēmatisei) our lowly body (sōma tapeinōseōs) to be like his glorious body (symmorphon tō sōmati tēs doxēs autou), by the power that enables him even to subject all things to himself.' Metaschēmatisei (will transform the outward configuration of — cf. schēma in 2:8). The resurrection body of Christ (sōma doxēs) is the template; the transformation is accomplished by the same power that subjects all things (ta panta) — the universal sovereignty of the exalted Christ. The eschatological end of the letter returns to the Christ-hymn's exaltation language.

1.1: Paulos kai Timotheos douloi Christou Iesou pasin tois hagiois en Christo Iesou tois ousin en Philippois syn episkopois kai diakonois: Paul and Timothy, douloi Christou Iesou (slaves of Christ Jesus). The self-designation doulos (slave) is Paul's strongest claim of belonging and subordination — the slave has no autonomous will. Episkopois kai diakonois (overseers and deacons) — this is the earliest datable use of these two terms together as distinct offices. Episkopos (overseer) denotes supervisory leadership; diakonos (servant/minister/deacon) denotes functional service. Philippians is uniquely addressed to the leaders alongside the whole congregation.
1.2: charis hymin kai eirene apo theou patros hemon kai kyriou Iesou Christou: Grace (charis) and peace (eirēnē) — the Pauline greeting fusing Greek (chairein) and Hebrew (shalom) in a theologically loaded form. The dual source (God the Father and Lord Jesus Christ) is Paul's standard binitarian greeting-formula, asserting that the grace and peace flow from both Father and Son as a unified divine source.
1.3: eucharisto to theo mou epi pase te mneia hymon: 'I thank my God in all my remembrance of you' — the thanksgiving period (1:3-11) is one of Paul's warmest. Eucharistō (I give thanks) is the Greek verb from which eucharist derives. The possessive to theo mou (my God) reflects the personal relationship language of the Psalms.
1.4: pantote en pase deēsei mou hyper pantōn hymōn meta charas tēn deesin poioumenos: 'always in every prayer of mine for you all, making my prayer with joy' — deēsis (petition, prayer-request) and chara (joy) are the twin notes of the letter's opening. Joy (chara/chairō) appears 16 times in Philippians — the most joy-saturated letter in the Pauline corpus, written from prison.
1.5: epi tē koinōnia hymōn eis to euangelion apo tēs prōtēs hēmeras achri tou nyn: 'because of your koinōnia in the gospel from the first day until now.' Koinōnia (partnership, participation, fellowship) is the letter's key relational term. It denotes not merely emotional fellowship but active sharing — the Philippians' financial partnership in Paul's missionary work (4:15-17: no church shared with me in giving and receiving except you) and their participation in the gospel project itself. Euangelion (gospel, good news) is the content of the partnership.
1.6: pepoithōs auto touto hoti ho enarcxamenos en hymin ergon agathon epitelesei achri hēmeras Christou Iesou: 'being confident of this very thing, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Christ Jesus.' The eschatological confidence is grounded in divine faithfulness: God who initiated (ho enarcxamenos) will also complete (epitelesei). The hēmera Christou Iesou (day of Christ Jesus) — the parousia as the telos of the divine work — appears 3 times in vv.6-11.
1.7: kathōs estin dikaion emoi touto phronein hyper pantōn hymōn dia to echein me en tē kardia hymas: 'It is right for me to feel this way about you all, because I hold you in my heart.' Phronein (to think, feel, have this mindset) — the key verb of Philippians, appearing 10 times. Kardia (heart) as the seat of commitment and will. Both in my imprisonment and in the defense (apologia) and confirmation (bebaiōsis) of the gospel, you are all partakers (synkoinōnous) with me of grace.
1.8: martys gar mou ho theos hōs epipothō pantas hymas en splanchnois Christou Iesou: 'For God is my witness, how I yearn for you all with the affection (splanchnois) of Christ Jesus.' Splanchnon (bowels, gut, deep affection) was the ancient term for the seat of compassion — the visceral center of feeling. The genitive Christou Iesou is significant: Paul's yearning is the yearning of Christ himself, not merely Paul's own affection. The apostle has become so identified with Christ that Christ's own compassion flows through him.
1.9: kai touto proseuchomai hina hē agapē hymōn eti mallon kai mallon perisseuē en epignōsei kai pasē aisthēsei: 'And it is my prayer that your love may abound more and more, with epignōsis (full/genuine knowledge) and all discernment (aisthēsis).' Epignōsis (epi + gnōsis) is the intensified knowledge — thorough, personal, relational knowing rather than mere intellectual acquaintance. Aisthēsis (moral perception, discernment) — the trained capacity to recognize what is genuinely good (v.10: dokimazein ta diapheronta — to approve what is excellent).
1.10: eis to dokimazein hymas ta diapheronta hina ēte eilikrineis kai aproskopoi eis hēmeral Christou: 'so that you may approve what is excellent (ta diapheronta), and so be pure and blameless for the day of Christ.' Dokimazein (to test, approve through testing) — the word for assaying metal. Ta diapheronta (the things that differ, the excellent things) — Paul prays for the Philippians' moral discernment to identify what genuinely matters among competing goods. Eilikrineis (pure, sincere — possibly from eilē + krinō, judged in sunlight): transparent under examination.
1.11: peplērōmenoi karpon dikaiosynēs ton dia Iesou Christou eis doxan kai epainon theou: 'filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God.' Karpos dikaiosynēs (fruit of righteousness) echoes the prophetic tradition (Amos 6:12 LXX; Prov 11:30; Isa 32:17: the fruit of righteousness will be peace). The righteousness is not earned by the Philippians but comes through Jesus Christ — its source and channel is christological. Its goal: God's glory and praise.
1.12: ginōskein de hymas boulomai adelphoi hoti ta kat' eme mallon eis prokopēn tou euangeliou elēlythen: 'I want you to know, brothers, that what has happened to me has really served to advance (prokopēn) the gospel.' Prokopē (progress, advance) was a Stoic philosophical term for moral progress toward the ideal sage. Paul uses it for the gospel's advance — the unexpected instrument being his imprisonment. The paradox: chains became a pulpit.
1.13: hōste tous desmous mou phaneroys en Christō genesthai en holō tō praitōriō kai tois loipois pasin: 'so that it has become known throughout the whole praetorian guard and to all the rest that my imprisonment is for Christ.' Praitorion (Praetorium) — most naturally the Praetorian Guard headquarters in Rome. En Christō (in/for Christ) — the chains are christologically defined: not punishment but martyrdom. The Praetorian soldiers knew why Paul was imprisoned; the gospel explanation defined the captivity.
1.14: kai tous pleionas tōn adelphōn en kyriō pepoithotas tois desmois mou perissoteros tolman adeoōs ton logon lalein: 'and most of the brothers, having become confident in the Lord by my imprisonment, are much more bold to speak the word without fear.' The apostle's imprisonment emboldened rather than silenced the broader community. The gospel's advance came through Paul's chains AND through those his chains inspired. Adeoōs (fearlessly, without hesitation) — the courage directly proportional to the apostle's willingness to suffer.
1.15: tines men kai dia phthonon kai erin, tines de kai di' eudokian ton Christon kēryssousin: 'Some indeed preach Christ from envy and rivalry, but others from good will.' The remarkable acknowledgment: Christ is preached with mixed motives — envy, rivalry, faction. Phthonos (envy) and eris (strife) were standard vices in Greco-Roman catalogues. Paul does not identify these preachers (rival Paulinists? Jewish-Christian missionaries?); the point is their motivation, not their theology.
1.16: hoi men ex agapēs eidotes hoti eis apologian tou euangeliou keimai: 'the latter do it out of love, knowing that I am put here for the defense of the gospel.' Apologia (defense, legal defense) — the term for a formal judicial defense speech. Paul frames his imprisonment as an ongoing legal defense of the gospel before Roman authorities. Those who preach from love understand this; those who preach from rivalry are, by contrast, presenting Paul's imprisonment as his failure.
1.17: hoi de ex eritheias ton Christon katangellousin ouch hagnōs, oiomenoi thlipsin egeirein tois desmois mou: 'the former proclaim Christ out of selfish ambition (eritheias), not sincerely, thinking to afflict me in my imprisonment.' Eritheia (selfish ambition, factional self-seeking) — originally the term for day-labor for hire, suggesting mercenary motivation. These preachers apparently believe their success embarrasses the imprisoned Paul or proves his methods deficient.
1.18: ti gar; plēn hoti panti tropō, eite prophasei eite alētheia, Christos katangelletai, kai en toutō chairō: 'What then? Only that in every way, whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is proclaimed, and in that I rejoice.' The striking pastoral magnanimity: Paul rejoices that Christ is preached even by those whose motives he questions. The object of proclamation — Christ — is what matters, not the purity of the proclaimer's motives. Yes, and I will rejoice — chairō shifts to the future: the joy is not circumstance-dependent.
1.19: oida gar hoti touto moi apobēsetai eis sōtērian dia tēs hymōn deēseōs kai epichorēgias tou pneumatos Iēsou Christou: 'for I know that through your prayers and the help (epichorēgia) of the Spirit of Jesus Christ this will turn out for my deliverance (sōtērian).' Epichorēgia (supply, provision, full equipping) — originally the wealthy citizen's sponsorship of a chorus. The Spirit's provision is bountiful supply. Sōtēria here likely means both immediate rescue and eschatological salvation — Paul is content with either outcome (vv.20-26).
1.20: kata tēn apokaradokian kai elpida mou hoti en oudeni aiscynthēsomai alla en pasē parrēsia hōs pantote kai nyn megalynthēsetai Christos en tō sōmati mou: 'as it is my eager expectation (apokaradokia) and hope that I will not be at all ashamed, but that with full courage (parrēsia) now as always Christ will be honored (megalynthēsetai) in my body.' Apokaradokia (eager expectation, neck-craning anticipation) — a Pauline compound (Rom 8:19 also) conveying intense forward-straining. Christ honored/magnified in Paul's body — whether through life or death (v.20b); the body is the apostolic field of Christological display.
1.21: emoi gar to zēn Christos kai to apothanein kerdos: 'For to me to live is Christ and to die is gain.' The most compressed summary of Paul's existential situation. To zēn Christos: life = Christ, not a christological supplement to normal life but its complete content. To apothanein kerdos: death = gain — not because life is bad but because death means fuller presence with Christ (v.23). The equation is not escapist but participatory: life fully in Christ, death more fully in Christ.
1.22: ei de to zēn en sarki, touto moi karpos ergou: 'If I am to live in the flesh, that means fruitful labor for me.' The tension between the two goods: continued life produces fruit (karpos ergou) for the congregation; death produces gain for Paul. The apostolic vocation suspends personal preference: the congregation's need takes priority over personal desire (v.24: to remain in the flesh is more necessary for your sake).
1.23: synechomai de ek tōn dyo, tēn epithymian echōn eis to analyein kai syn Christō einai, pollō [gar] mallon kreisson: 'I am hard pressed between the two. My desire is to depart (analyein) and be with Christ, for that is far better.' Synechomai (to be pressed, held in by, constrained) — Paul is caught between two goods. Analyein (to break camp, weigh anchor, depart) — the departure image evokes a ship leaving harbor. Syn Christō einai (to be with Christ) — the goal of death for believers: personal presence with Christ, not mere dissolution.
1.24: to de epimenein [en] tē sarki anagkaioteron di' hymas: 'But to remain in the flesh is more necessary on your account.' The apostle's survival calculus is entirely other-directed: not self-preservation or self-interest but the congregation's need. The pastoral logic of cruciform love: the apostle's continued life is a gift given to the congregation, not a personal preference indulged.
1.25: kai touto pepoithōs oida hoti menō kai paramenō pasin hymin eis tēn hymōn prokopēn kai charan tēs pisteōs: 'Convinced of this, I know that I will remain and continue with you all, for your progress (prokopēn) and joy in the faith.' Prokopē again — the Philippians' advance in faith and joy is the purpose of Paul's continued ministry. The joy (chara tēs pisteōs — joy of faith) encompasses both the apostle's joy and the congregation's joy in the faith they share.
1.26: hina to kauchēma hymōn perisseuē en Christō Iēsou en emoi dia tēs emēs parousias palin pros hymas: 'so that in me you may have ample cause to glory in Christ Jesus, because of my coming (parousias) to you again.' Kauchēma (boast, ground of boasting) — in Christ Jesus: the boasting is not in Paul himself but in Christ through Paul. Parousia (presence, coming) — the same word used for Christ's eschatological coming; Paul's anticipated visit is a mini-parousia of the apostle to his congregation.
1.27: monon axiōs tou euangeliou tou Christou politeuesthe: 'Only let your manner of life (politeuesthe) be worthy of the gospel of Christ.' Politeuesthai (to live as a citizen, to conduct oneself as a member of a polity) — the civic-political verb from polis (city). The Philippians as Roman colonists understood citizenship obligations; Paul redirects their civic identity: the gospel defines the polity in which they live. This verse is the hinge from personal matters (vv.12-26) to community exhortation (1:27-4:9).
1.28: kai mē ptyromenoi en mēdeni hypo tōn antikeimenōn: 'and not frightened in anything by your opponents.' Ptyromenoi (frightened, startled — used of horses shying) — the congregation's steadfastness is itself a sign (endeixis): to the opponents it signals destruction; to the Philippians it signals their salvation, and this from God. The courage of the congregation in the face of opposition is itself apologetic witness.
1.29: hoti hymin echaristhē to hyper Christou, ou monon to eis auton pisteuein alla kai to hyper autou paschein: 'For it has been granted (echaristhē — a grace-gift) to you that for the sake of Christ you should not only believe in him but also suffer for his sake.' The remarkable claim: suffering for Christ is a charisma (grace-gift), not a punishment or failure. The suffering and the faith are equally granted by divine grace — both are God's gifts to the congregation.
1.30: ton auton agōna echontes hoion eidete en emoi kai nyn akouete en emoi: 'engaged in the same conflict (agōna) that you saw I had and now hear that I still have.' Agōn (contest, struggle — from the athletic and gladiatorial arena) — the shared struggle of apostle and congregation. The Philippians saw Paul's first conflict when he was imprisoned in Philippi (Acts 16:19-24); now they hear of his Roman imprisonment. The apostolic pattern of suffering is the congregation's own pattern.

4.1: hoste, adelphoi mou agapetoi kai epipotheto, chara kai stephanos mou: 'therefore' (hoste) draws the conclusion of 3:17-21. Agapetoi kai epipotheto (beloved and longed for): the doubled emotional language is unusually warm. Stephanos (crown): the victor's wreath of the athletic games, not the royal diadem (diadem). Paul uses the same crown-image in 1 Thess 2:19-20 where the community itself is his crown at Christ's Parousia. The crown is eschatological — not present status but anticipated reward. Stekete en Kyrio: stand firm in the Lord — the imperative that 3:20-21's Parousia hope grounds.
4.2: Euodian parakaleo kai Syntichen parakaleo to auto phronein en Kyrio: the only named individuals addressed in the main body of the letter (apart from Epaphroditus and Timothy). Both names are Greek — Euodia (Good Journey) and Syntyche (Happy Chance). The identical formula applied to each (parakaleo...) gives neither precedence. The dispute between them is not specified. To auto phronein (be of the same mind): the phronein root is the letter's key term — phronein en Kyrio means having the same Christ-oriented mindset that Paul described in 2:5 (let this mind be in you which was in Christ Jesus).
4.3: nai eroto kai se, gnesie syzyge: 'true/genuine companion' (gnesie syzyge). Gnesios (genuine, dispute level 2): MKT 'true companion'; lit. legitimately born, genuine — the opposite of counterfeit. The identity of this 'true companion' is debated: Epaphroditus, Luke, Silas, or even Paul's wife (though the masculine adjective makes the last unlikely). Biblo tes zoes (book of life, dispute level 2 — zoe): the eschatological register of the righteous (Exod 32:32-33; Isa 4:3; Dan 12:1; Rev 3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:12-15).
4.4: chairete en Kyrio pantote: the double imperative rejoice — the repetition is emphatic. Chairō (rejoice/be glad): MKT 'rejoice'; the word family includes 'grace' (charis) and 'gift' (charisma) — all derived from the root of gladness/favor. The qualifier en Kyrio (in the Lord): rejoicing is not a psychological technique but a location — located within the sphere of Christ's lordship, which guarantees the Parousia-hope (3:20-21) and therefore grounds present joy regardless of circumstances (Paul writes from prison). Pantote (always): no circumstantial exception.
4.5: to epieikes hymon gnostheto pasin anthropois: let your epieikes be known to all people. Epieikes (gentleness, forbearance, reasonableness, moderation): one of the most difficult Greek virtue-terms to translate. It occurs in 1 Tim 3:3; Tit 3:2; James 3:17; 1 Pet 2:18. Aristotle defined it as equity that corrects strict legal justice when strict application would be unjust (Nicomachean Ethics 5.10). In the LXX it renders the Hebrew concept of patient, non-retaliatory response. MKT 'gentleness' captures one dimension. Ho Kyrios eggys: the Lord is near (eggys — close, proximate). Ambiguous: spatial (the Lord is close — omnipresent) or temporal (the Parousia is imminent, cf. 3:20-21; 1 Cor 16:22; Rev 22:20). The temporal reading connects to the entire eschatological logic of ch 3-4.
4.6: meden merimnate: be anxious about nothing (merimnaō — to be distracted, divided in one's concerns, pulled in different directions). The same verb family appears in Matt 6:25-34 (do not worry) and Luke 10:41 (Martha is worried/distracted about many things). En panti...: in every situation, by prayer (proseuche — general prayer), petition (deesis — specific request), and thanksgiving (eucharistia) — three modes of address to God. The thanksgiving embedded in request is the Philippians' characteristic posture (1:3). To aitema hymon (your requests): the content of the petitions presented before God.
4.7: he eirene tou theou he hyperechousa panta noun phrouresei tas kardias hymon kai ta noemata hymon en Christo Iesou: the peace of God surpassing all understanding will guard your hearts and minds. Eirene (peace): the Greek rendering of Hebrew shalom — comprehensive well-being, not merely absence of conflict. Hyperechousa panta noun: surpassing (hyperecho — to excel, be superior to) all mind/understanding. Phroureō (guard): military verb — to stand sentinel, post a guard; used for soldiers guarding a city gate or a prisoner. The peace of God is personified as a military guard standing watch over the hearts and minds. En Christo Iesou: in Christ Jesus — the sphere within which the guarding operates.
4.8: hosa estin alethē, hosa semna, hosa dikaia, hosa hagna, hosa prosphile, hosa euphema: the virtue catalogue of six hosa (whatever) clauses. Semna (noble/honorable/worthy of respect): the adjective for what commands reverence — used in 1 Tim 3:8, 11; Tit 2:2. Arete (virtue, excellence): the only occurrence of this key Stoic philosophical term in Paul. Arete is the central concept of Greek ethics — the excellence proper to a thing or person (Aristotle); the four cardinal virtues of Stoicism. Paul uses it without apology, demonstrating that Greek ethical vocabulary can serve Christian formation. Epainos (praise, commendation): what merits approval. The list moves from ontological qualities (true, noble, right, pure) to aesthetic ones (lovely, admirable) to the encompassing virtue-term and the functional criterion (praise).
4.9: ha kai emathete kai parelabete kai ekousate kai eidete en emoi: four verbs of reception — learned, received, heard, seen. Parelabete (received): the technical term for tradition-transmission (1 Cor 11:23; 15:3) — the Philippians received the apostolic tradition from Paul. Ekousate (heard): auditory reception of Paul's teaching. Eidete en emoi (seen in me): the apostle himself is the embodied example of the teaching — his conduct is the lived tradition the community observed. Tauta prassete: practice these things. Ho de theos tes eirenes estai meth' hymon: the God of peace (cf. the peace of God, v.7) will be with them — the divine presence as the consequence of Christ-shaped practice.
4.10: Echarēn de en Kyrio megalos: I rejoiced greatly in the Lord — the joy-theme (v.4) applied to Paul's own experience. Hoti ede pote anethaleite to hyper emou phronein: that your thinking-for-me has revived (anathallo — to bloom again, revive; used of plants putting out new growth). Phronein hyper emou (thinking/caring for me): the phronein root again — the letter's key term now applied to the community's practical concern for Paul. Ef' ho kai ephroneit' akaireisthe de: indeed you were thinking of me — you lacked the opportunity. Paul preemptively softens any implication of neglect: they were concerned but circumstantially unable to help.
4.11: ouch hoti ephzeto to hysterema lego: not that I speak from need (hysterema — what is lacking, the deficit). Ego gar emathon en hois eimi autarkes einai: I have learned (emathon — aorist, completed learning) to be content (autarkes) in whatever circumstances. Autarkes (self-sufficient, content): the Stoic term for self-sufficiency — the wise person's independence from external circumstances. The Stoic autarkeia was achieved through the development of inner rational virtue. Paul uses the vocabulary but fundamentally reorients it: he has learned contentment, not achieved it through native virtue; and the sufficiency comes from Christ who strengthens him (v.13), not from inner resources.
4.12: oida kai tapeinousthai, oida kai perisseuein: I know how to be humbled (tapeinoomai — the verb of Christ's kenosis in 2:8) and how to abound. En panti kai en pasin memyemai: in all things and in all circumstances I have been initiated (myeō — perfect passive: to initiate into a mystery; to instruct in the secrets). Myeō is the technical vocabulary of Hellenistic mystery religions: to be initiated into the sacred rites. Paul adopts the vocabulary while inverting the source: he was 'initiated' not by a mystery cult but by Christ — the 'secret' (mysterion) of contentment through Christ's empowering. The verb appears only here in the NT.
4.13: panta ischyo en to endynamounti me Christo: I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me. Ischyo (to be strong, able, avail): general strength-capability. Endynamoō (strengthen): the en-prefix intensifying dynamoō (to empower from dynamis — power). The verse is directly connected to the contentment claim of vv.11-12: 'all things' refers to all the circumstances of abasement and abundance just listed, not an unlimited universal capability. The strengthening comes from Christ himself — the one who 'works in you to will and to act' (2:13: ho theos estin ho energōn en hymin).
4.14: plen kalos epoiesate synkoinonesantes mou te thlipsei: yet it was well done of you to share (synkoinoneo — to participate together with, to co-fellowship) in my affliction (thlipsis). The syn-prefix in synkoinoneo recurs throughout Philippians — the sharing and participation that characterizes authentic community (1:7: synkoinonous mou tes charitos; 1:5: koinonia eis to euangelion). Even as Paul distances himself from material need (vv.11-12), he values the Philippians' participation itself as a relational act of solidarity.
4.15: oidate de kai hymeis, Philippesioi, hoti en arche tou euangeliou: the Philippians know their own history with Paul. Arche tou euangeliou (the beginning of the gospel): Paul's first missionary campaign in Europe, ~49-50 CE. Logon doseos kai lempseos: the accounting of giving and receiving (logos in the commercial/bookkeeping sense — the account balance). Logos (dispute level 2): here clearly the financial-accounting sense (credit/debit ledger), not the theological Word. Only the Philippian church had a giving-and-receiving relationship with Paul — they were his unique financial partners in mission.
4.16: hoti kai en Thessalonike kai hapax kai dis eis ten chreian moi epempsate: even when I was in Thessalonica, you sent to my need (chreia) more than once (hapax kai dis — once and twice = repeatedly). This is confirmed by 1 Thess 2:9 (Paul worked with his hands in Thessalonica) and Acts 17:1-9 (brief Thessalonian ministry). The Philippian support supplemented Paul's manual labor income during the Thessalonian mission. The historicity of the detail grounds the gratitude in specific remembered acts of generosity.
4.17: ouch hoti epizeteo to doma, alla epizeteo ton karpont on pleonazonta eis logon hymon: not that I seek the gift (doma — the concrete transfer of goods), but I seek the fruit (karpos) that increases to your account (logos — the ledger entry). Logos (dispute level 2): again the commercial-accounting sense. The eschatological reversal: Paul does not want his financial account credited but theirs. Their generosity will accumulate in the divine ledger as credit toward the eschatological judgment (cf. 2 Cor 9:6-11; Matt 6:20: store up treasures in heaven).
4.18: apecho de panta kai perisseuō: I have received full payment (apecho — the technical commercial receipt verb, used on papyri receipts meaning 'paid in full'; cf. Matt 6:2, 5, 16 where the hypocrites 'have received their reward' — apechousin). Paul issues a formal receipt. Osme euodias, thysian dekten, euareston to theo: a pleasing aroma (euodia), an acceptable sacrifice (thysia dekte), well-pleasing to God (euarestos). The sacrificial-cultic vocabulary comes from the LXX: osme euodias appears in Gen 8:21 (Noah's offering) and repeatedly in Exod-Lev for acceptable burnt offerings. The Philippians' financial gift is reframed as a priestly offering ascending to God.
4.19: ho de theos mou plerose pasan chreian hymon kata to ploutos autou en doxe en Christo Iesou: my God will supply (pleroō — fill full, satisfy completely) all your need (chreian — the same word used of Paul's need in v.16) according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus. The logical reciprocity: they supplied Paul's need; God will supply theirs. Kata to ploutos autou en doxe: according to his wealth/riches in glory — not a miserly minimum but a supply proportioned to divine abundance. En Christo Iesou: the Christ-sphere is the medium through which God's supply comes.
4.20: to de theo kai patri hemon he doxa eis tous aionas ton aionon. amen: the doxology. To theo kai patri hemon: to our God and Father — the letter's characteristic double designation (1:2: God our Father). He doxa (the glory): doxa (dispute in wider vocabulary; here unambiguous doxological use). Eis tous aionas ton aionon (unto the ages of the ages): the Hebrew idiom of temporal infinity — the Amen-formula closes the letter's body before the final greetings.
4.21: aspasasthe panta hagion en Christo Iesou: greet every saint in Christ Jesus. Hagion (holy one, saint — hagios, dispute level 2): those set apart for God. Hoi syn emoi adelphoi: the brothers with Paul — his co-workers in the place of imprisonment, probably Rome.
4.22: aspazontai hymas pantes hoi hagioi, malista de hoi ek tes Kaisaros oikias: all the saints greet you, especially those from Caesar's household (Kaisaros oikia). Kaisaros oikia is not the imperial family but the vast network of slaves and freedmen who administered Roman imperial operations — found in cities throughout the empire. Inscriptional evidence (CIL VI) documents hundreds of imperial slaves and freedmen at Rome. Their greetings from Rome to Philippi — a Roman colony — represent the gospel's penetration into the imperial administrative apparatus, a remarkable irony given that Paul writes from Roman custody.
4.23: he charis tou Kyriou Iesou Christou meta tou pneumatos hymon: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit (pneuma). The singular pneumatos hymon (your spirit — singular, not plural 'spirits') treats the community as a single collective person, or uses spirit for the inner life of each member. The closing grace-benediction is Pauline standard (Gal 6:18; Phlm 25; 1 Cor 16:23; 2 Cor 13:14); here the spirit/grace pairing connects to the Spirit-Christ relationship throughout the letter. Amen: the liturgical assent that closes the letter in public worship.
